author: @Earzone63
text: Recently I bought 2 TEGs / Peltiers because i was planning to try the equivalent of a 'Thermopile' (Inspired by the lemon battery 'bank' / Voltaic pile), but still considering how best to pull off a hot-cold-hot-cold type of design without too much leakage .. although just thinking about it now 1) just turn one over and spread them out spatially,  2) create a 'sandwich' of devices H-C C-H H-C    ...or 3) some funky shaped beaker for these exothermic reactions lol

author: @David_Mash
text: Does the chalk degrade as the water evaporates? Or can a constant water source maintain heat

replies.author: @kateonianlaw1127
replies.text: An answer to your question is that calcium oxide turns in calcium hydroxide (slaked lime) by adding water, which in turn, the wet calcium hydroxide slowly turns into calcium carbonate by absorbing carbon dioxide from the air.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Kateonian law gave a good answer mate

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Although all are excellent, I like this principle more than your prior "emergency power" demonstrations.  The reall nice thing about this is that a real thermal battery setup could generate power even while the lime was being heated in the "charging" state -- so long as the other terminal could be cooled.   One could supply thermal solar for generation and recharge with wind-powered pneumatic cooling for the "cold sink," and still retain a constant output.  A sensor could add water when thermal heating was not available.  Great demonstration, Robert!

author: @Mangomesh
text: This is a bit random, I'd like to know what I can do with my egg shells? Has anyone found a use for them, I really hate just throwing them in the trash.

replies.author: @brettmoore3194
replies.text: If you have excess vinegar,acetic acid, you could make calcium acetate. Once heated if you capture the fumes of the heated calcium acetate it will produce acetone and calcium oxide

replies.author: @Charlie-Oooooo
replies.text: May I suggest composting? If you added  perished leftovers, rotten fruit, coffee grinds, etc., you could generate some nice nutrients for your yard or garden (or someone else's). There's many container systems for sale around with many sizes that do it in some logical way. In fact, I've wondered if communities will someday adopt a compost collection service. Think of all the good nutrients that go to waste in all the garbage dumps. Not sure exactly, but it probably accounts for about 1/4 of all landfills. Just a thought :)

replies.author: @Mangomesh
replies.text: @Charlie-Oooooo Interesting, whats the best method of grinding the egg shells up? I remember a family member telling me that egg shells take a long time to break down into compost.

replies.author: @Charlie-Oooooo
replies.text: @Mangomesh If you just break them up by hand that's fine as they will break down eventually, wherever they might wind up. If you really need to, you could use a small coffee bean grinder, but you might have to allocate it solely for eggshells. Cheers :)
